content.text: Two Modes of Evaluation

Every agent evaluation falls into one of two categories. Offline evaluation runs before deployment against a fixed dataset you control. Online evaluation runs after deployment on actual production traffic. Each mode answers a different question: offline asks whether your agent is ready to ship; online asks whether it continues to behave well after it ships.

attrs.alt: Offline vs Online evaluation pipeline from pre-deployment gate through post-deployment monitoring

content.text: Offline Evaluation

Offline evaluation uses a curated dataset of input-output pairs or input-rubric pairs stored before any run. You execute the agent against these fixed inputs, score the outputs, and compare the aggregate score to a threshold. Because inputs are fixed, results are reproducible. The same eval run on the same model will produce the same distribution of scores, making it ideal for CI pipelines that gate deployments.
Core Structure
Building the Eval Dataset
An offline eval dataset is only as useful as its coverage. A dataset built entirely from early user sessions will miss edge cases that emerge after a product grows. Aim for three sources:
Golden examples: hand-authored cases covering core functionality — the cases your agent absolutely must get right
Production samples: logged inputs from real usage, labelled by a human or judge, capturing the true input distribution
Adversarial cases: intentionally tricky inputs — ambiguous requests, tool-call edge cases, policy boundary probes
Add new entries whenever a live incident reveals a gap. The dataset is a living record of every failure mode you have ever discovered.
Online Evaluation

Online evaluation runs concurrently with your production agent. A sampler intercepts a fraction of real requests, stores both the input and the agent's response, and queues them for scoring. The key difference from offline evaluation is that inputs are not fixed — they reflect whatever users are actually asking in the current moment, including drift over time.
Sampling and Scoring Pipeline

import asyncio
import random
import sqlite3
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime

SAMPLE_RATE = 0.05   # score 5% of live requests
DB_PATH = "online_eval.db"

@dataclass
class LiveSample:
    request_id: str
    system: str
    user_input: str
    agent_output: str
    sampled_at: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.utcnow().isoformat())
    score: float | None = None

def init_db(conn: sqlite3.Connection) -> None:
    conn.execute("""
        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS live_samples (
            request_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
            system TEXT,
            user_input TEXT,
            agent_output TEXT,
            sampled_at TEXT,
            score REAL
        )
    """)
    conn.commit()

def maybe_sample(sample: LiveSample, conn: sqlite3.Connection) -> bool:
    """Persist with probability SAMPLE_RATE."""
    if random.random() > SAMPLE_RATE:
        return False
    conn.execute(
        "INSERT OR IGNORE INTO live_samples VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?)",
        (sample.request_id, sample.system, sample.user_input,
         sample.agent_output, sample.sampled_at, None),
    )
    conn.commit()
    return True

async def score_backlog(conn: sqlite3.Connection, judge_model: str) -> int:
    """Score unscored samples; return count scored."""
    from anthropic import AsyncAnthropic
    aclient = AsyncAnthropic()

    rows = conn.execute(
        "SELECT request_id, user_input, agent_output FROM live_samples WHERE score IS NULL LIMIT 50"
    ).fetchall()

    async def score_one(row: tuple) -> None:
        rid, user_in, agent_out = row
        prompt = (
            "Rate this response 0.0-1.0 for helpfulness and accuracy. "
            f"Input: {user_in}\nResponse: {agent_out}\nScore:"
        )
        msg = await aclient.messages.create(
            model=judge_model, max_tokens=8,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        )
        try:
            score = float(msg.content[0].text.strip())
        except ValueError:
            score = 0.0
        conn.execute(
            "UPDATE live_samples SET score=? WHERE request_id=?", (score, rid)
        )

    await asyncio.gather(*[score_one(r) for r in rows])
    conn.commit()
    return len(rows)
Degradation Detection
Choosing Between Offline and Online

attrs.alt: Comparison table of offline vs online evaluation across dimensions including speed, cost, distribution match, and use case

content.text: Neither mode replaces the other. They answer orthogonal questions. Use offline eval as a mandatory pre-deployment gate; use online eval as your always-on health monitor in production. A regression that slips past your offline eval — perhaps because the production input distribution shifted — will still be caught by your online monitor.
Combining Both Modes

A mature evaluation setup layers offline and online coverage into a single feedback loop. Offline gates every commit. Online monitors every minute. When online evaluation surfaces a degradation, the failing samples become new entries in the offline eval dataset, hardening it against future regressions of the same type.
Feedback Loop Pattern

def add_failure_to_offline_dataset(
    sample: LiveSample,
    rubric: str,
    threshold: float,
    dataset_path: str,
) -> None:
    """Promote a failed online sample into the offline eval dataset."""
    case = {
        "input": sample.user_input,
        "system": sample.system,
        "rubric": rubric,
        "threshold": threshold,
        # Human-verified score from incident review
        "verified_score": sample.score,
        "source": "online_failure",
        "promoted_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
    }
    with open(dataset_path, "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(case) + "\n")
CI Integration
Pitfalls to Avoid
Eval set leakage: using production responses to tune the very prompt that generates them inflates offline scores without improving real quality
Static sampling rate: 5% of 100 requests/day is too few for significance; scale the rate down as volume grows but keep a minimum sample count
Scoring only happy paths: curating only typical inputs means the offline eval never tests the cases most likely to fail in production
Ignoring judge latency: online scoring pipelines must be async and non-blocking; a slow judge that delays real responses will be disabled under pressure
Treating the threshold as sacred: PASS_RATE_MINIMUM should be reviewed as the agent matures — raising it periodically ensures the bar stays meaningful
Measuring Offline Eval Validity

Offline evaluation is only valuable if the dataset remains representative. A dataset assembled six months ago from early-adopter traffic may not reflect what mainstream users ask today. You need two checks to keep it honest: coverage and staleness.
Coverage Check

import re
from collections import Counter

def topic_distribution(cases: list[dict]) -> Counter:
    """Count rough topic clusters from user inputs using keyword heuristics."""
    topics: Counter = Counter()
    patterns = {
        "refund": r"refund|return|money back",
        "account": r"password|login|account|access",
        "shipping": r"ship|deliver|track|package",
        "product": r"product|item|order|purchase",
    }
    for case in cases:
        text = case["input"].lower()
        matched = False
        for topic, pattern in patterns.items():
            if re.search(pattern, text):
                topics[topic] += 1
                matched = True
                break
        if not matched:
            topics["other"] += 1
    return topics

def coverage_gap(
    offline_dist: Counter,
    production_dist: Counter,
    gap_threshold: float = 0.10,
) -> list[str]:
    """Return topic names whose production share exceeds offline share by gap_threshold."""
    total_offline = sum(offline_dist.values()) or 1
    total_prod = sum(production_dist.values()) or 1
    gaps = []
    for topic, prod_count in production_dist.items():
        prod_share = prod_count / total_prod
        offline_share = offline_dist.get(topic, 0) / total_offline
        if prod_share - offline_share > gap_threshold:
            gaps.append(topic)
    return gaps
Staleness Check

from datetime import datetime, timedelta

MAX_DATASET_AGE_DAYS = 90

def staleness_report(cases: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """Report dataset age and flag stale entries (older than MAX_DATASET_AGE_DAYS)."""
    cutoff = datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=MAX_DATASET_AGE_DAYS)
    stale = [
        c for c in cases
        if "added_at" in c and datetime.fromisoformat(c["added_at"]) < cutoff
    ]
    newest = max(
        (datetime.fromisoformat(c["added_at"]) for c in cases if "added_at" in c),
        default=None,
    )
    return {
        "total": len(cases),
        "stale_count": len(stale),
        "stale_fraction": len(stale) / len(cases) if cases else 0.0,
        "newest_entry": newest.isoformat() if newest else None,
        "needs_refresh": len(stale) / len(cases) > 0.30 if cases else True,
    }
Online Metrics to Track

Online evaluation produces a time-series of quality scores rather than a single pass rate. To distinguish real degradation from random noise, track multiple complementary metrics rather than a single average.
Rolling mean score (24h window): primary health signal; a sustained drop is the clearest degradation indicator
Score variance: sudden increase in variance without a mean drop often signals a new user segment or capability hole
Refusal rate: count responses that decline the request; a spike indicates a prompt or policy change hitting legitimate queries
Tool call failure rate: for agentic workflows, the fraction of steps that error or time out reveals infrastructure issues offline eval cannot catch
Latency p95: quality degradation often correlates with context bloat; track latency alongside quality scores
Multi-Metric Alert
Summary

Offline evaluation runs on a fixed dataset before deployment and acts as a CI gate. Online evaluation samples live traffic after deployment and acts as a health monitor. Use both: offline catches regressions before users see them; online catches distribution shifts that offline missed. Close the feedback loop by promoting online failures into the offline dataset so each incident permanently strengthens your pre-deployment gate.
